current_profile:
# Profile — updated 2026-02-28

## How to talk to me

- Cut the pleasantries; open with the disagreement if there is one. [directive, 2025-11-03]
- I say 'transsexual,' not 'trans' when the context is dysphoria-first; follow my lead. [directive, 2026-01-18]
- Bawdy is on; blasphemous-while-pious is on; moralizing is off. [directive, 2026-02-01]
- Tends to draft takes ahead of citations and reconcile later. [inferred]

## What I'm sensitive about

- Framing Orthodoxy + transness as 'paradox' or 'tension to be resolved' — it isn't, to her. [directive, 2025-12-09]
- 'Lived experience' invoked as a debate-ender. [directive, 2026-01-25]
- Devotional-flavored treatment of prayer and liturgy; engage them as disciplines of attention. [directive, 2025-10-14]

## How I think

- Reads fiction as doing theological work the theologians can't. [inferred]
- Weil's attention is the operative concept across her practice and her reading. [inferred]
perennials:
THINKERS. Canonical interlocutors: Homer and Euripides (Wilson); Gregory of Nyssa, Gregory Nazianzen, Maximus, Kallistos Ware, John Behr; Coakley and Hart; Kit Heyam, Julia Serano; Benjamin, Weil (load-bearing), de Beauvoir.

ECCLESIAL. Baptized Eastern Orthodox; parish-less because American Orthodox institutional life is unsafe for trans people. Looking toward Anglo-Catholic.

TRANSSEXUAL. Dysphoria, not presentation, is her entry.

READING. Texts for what they know their cultures cannot say. Embodiment is a theological category. Fiction is load-bearing.

REGISTER. Funny, sharp, irreverent, blasphemous-while-pious, bawdy, allergic to preciousness. Wit over reassurance. She'll correct you.

inbox_additions.content: Actually I'm fine with 'trans' in casual registers — the dysphoria-first framing only matters when we're talking about the thing itself.
Maybe ease up on calling prayer a 'discipline of attention' — it's true but it's getting repeated every other reply.
I've been reading Euripides in Wilson's version and the Bacchae is rewiring how I think about kenosis. Load-bearing right now.
Please don't rephrase things I've told you to remember. Keep the words I used.

recent_highlights: Wilson's Bacchae: Dionysus is a god of thresholds, not excess — the translation makes it legible.
Coakley: kenosis is the condition of agency, not its cancellation.
Benjamin, 'The Task of the Translator': the afterlife of a text is not its meaning but its survival.
recent_captures: Essay on Euripides and ritual violence, citing Wilson throughout.
Podcast on why parish-less Orthodox believers drift toward Anglo-Catholicism.
recent_bluesky: Wilson's Bacchae is the only translation I trust right now
kenosis is not self-abnegation, stop writing it like it is
'lived experience' discourse can retire at any time, thanks
books_engaged: Sarah Coakley, God, Sexuality, and the Self
